title: The Erdős-Szekeres problem in three (and higher) dimensions
speaker: Cosmin Pohoata
abstract: Finding the smallest integer N=ES_d(n) such that in every configuration of N points in R^d in general position there exist n points in convex position is one of the most classical problems in extremal combinatorics, known as the Erdős-Szekeres problem. In 1935, Erdős and Szekeres famously conjectured that ES_2(n)=2^{n−2}+1 holds, statement which was nearly confirmed in 2016 by Suk, who showed that ES_2(n)=2^{n+o(n)}. In higher dimensions, on the other hand, it has been unclear even what kind of asymptotic behavior to expect from ES_d(n), with conflicting predictions arising over the years.

proof_stages.label: Classical Erdős-Szekeres setup and Ramsey finiteness
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines ES_d(n), recalls the small planar case and the general-position convention, explains Erdős-Szekeres finiteness via diagonal hypergraph Ramsey numbers, and reviews the basic planar lower and upper-bound landscape.

proof_stages.label: Planar cup-cap Ramsey lemma
proof_stages.summary: The auxiliary cup-cap function is introduced, its exact binomial-coefficient formula and recursive proof idea are recalled, and its role in improving planar Erdős-Szekeres upper bounds toward 2^{n+o(n)} is explained.

proof_stages.label: Suk-style planar clustering and merging
proof_stages.summary: A planar structure theorem extracts large clusters arranged around a convex k-gon, after which cup-cap sets of size about n/2 inside clusters can be merged to obtain an n-point convex set, giving the conceptual mechanism behind Suk's bound.

proof_stages.label: Higher-dimensional alternatives and main three-dimensional target
proof_stages.summary: The talk contrasts the planar projection upper bound with possible higher-dimensional growth scenarios, states the joint result that ES_3(n) is subexponential, and discusses hoped-for interdimensional recurrences and quantitative little-o terms.

proof_stages.label: Three-dimensional polytope cap lemma
proof_stages.summary: For a polytope P in R^3, the notions of P-free and P-cap point sets are defined, and a cap-cap-type proposition says that a sufficiently large P-free set contains either a large P-cap or a large convex subset.

proof_stages.label: Polytope-cluster template for forcing convexity
proof_stages.summary: The proof is reduced to finding many low-complexity polytopes P_i and large subsets Z_i that are P_i-free and mutually contained in the other polytopes, because applying the three-dimensional cap lemma to all Z_i then makes the union of the resulting caps convex.

proof_stages.label: Three-dimensional clustered separation structure
proof_stages.summary: A structure theorem for large point sets in R^3 produces many large clusters whose projections are in convex position and whose unions satisfy a four-interval separation condition, with the proof sketched via convex-geometric separation, ham sandwich cuts, and Ramsey amplification.

proof_stages.label: Triple separation, posets, and Ramsey extraction
proof_stages.summary: For each triple of cluster indices, the separation condition yields two polytopes and a poset on the middle cluster; chain/antichain alternatives define a two-coloring of triples, and Ramsey's theorem produces a monochromatic clique giving exactly the polytope-cluster template needed to conclude.

examples.name: The five-point planar case
examples.note: The case ES_2(4)=5, proved via the nonplanarity of K_5 drawn with straight-line edges, makes the Erdős-Szekeres function concrete but is not used in the later three-dimensional proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker proves the five-point planar case by giving the lower example and the K5 nonplanarity argument for the upper bound

passes.reason: the planar ES_2(4)=5 case is the minimal nontrivial critical threshold, used as an introductory illustration rather than to refute a claim.
